Job Board Effectiveness for College Students

Yasmeen R’ghioui

George Washington University

March 22, 2026

Table of contents

  • Overview
    • Offer Rate Based On Job Platform
    • Percentages of Students Who Receieved Offers vs. Students Who Didn’t
    • Offer Rate Based On Major and University Tier
    • Offer Salary Distribution Based On Major
    • Offer Rate Based On Number of Prior Internships
    • Offer Rate Across GPA Range
  • Final Reflection

Overview

How are job boards successful in employing college students? Are they even successful in employing college students? These are the main questions I will be exploring through different data analytical methods. This dataset was sourced from the Kaggle website. It was last updated on March 18th, 2026. It is updated annually. This dataset provides a comprehensive look at the platform efficacy of Handshake, LinkedIn, and Indeed for entry-level job seekers. It contains 100,000 records detailing student demographics (Major, University Tier, Region), academic performance (GPA, Internships), and explicitly tracks their application funnel (Applications Submitted -> First Round -> Second Round -> Offer). For students who secure an offer, the dataset includes target variables like Salary, Company Size, and Role Relevance. Through different forms of data visualization, I will examine which platforms, majors, and regions are most successful in getting students employed.

Code 
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import plotly.express as px
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
import calendar
data = pd.read_csv("job_data.csv")
Code 
#data cleaning
data['University_Rating'] = pd.Categorical(
    data['University_Rating'],
    categories=['Lower-tier', 'Mid-tier', 'Top-tier'],
    ordered=True
)

#data sorting
data.sort_values(['GPA', 'Offer_Salary'], ascending=[False, False], na_position='last').head(3)

#groupby
platform_stats = (
    data.groupby('Primary_Search_Platform', observed=True)
    .agg(
        offer_rate = ('Offer_Received',        'mean'),
        avg_apps   = ('Applications_Submitted', 'mean'),
        med_salary = ('Offer_Salary',           'median')
    )
    .round(2)
)

#reshaping
data_long = data[['Student_ID', 'Primary_Search_Platform',
              'Applications_Submitted', 'First_Round_Interviews', 'Second_Round_Interviews']
            ].melt(
    id_vars   = ['Student_ID', 'Primary_Search_Platform'],
    var_name  = 'Funnel_Stage',
    value_name= 'Count'
)

Offer Rate Based On Job Platform

This bar chart presents job offer rates by job platform. We can observe that Handshake and LinkedIn are tied with 37% of students who used each platform receiving a job offer. Indeed was ranked the lowest at just 26% of students receiving job offers.

Code 
platform_stats2 = (
    data.groupby('Primary_Search_Platform', observed=True)['Offer_Received']
    .mean()
    .reset_index()
    .sort_values('Offer_Received', ascending=False)
)

colors = ['#38f52f', '#2164f3', '#e8534a']

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5))

bars = ax.bar(
    platform_stats2['Primary_Search_Platform'],
    platform_stats2['Offer_Received'] * 100,
    color=colors,
    width=0.5,
    edgecolor='white'
)

for bar, val in zip(bars, platform_stats2['Offer_Received']):
    ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, bar.get_height() + 0.4,
            f'{val:.0%}', ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=11, fontweight='bold')

ax.set_title('Offer Rate by Job Platform', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold', pad=12)
ax.set_ylabel('Offer Rate (%)', fontsize=10)
ax.set_ylim(0, 50)
ax.spines[['top', 'right']].set_visible(False)
ax.tick_params(axis='x', labelsize=10)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('offer_rate_barplot.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

Percentages of Students Who Receieved Offers vs. Students Who Didn’t

This donut chart shows that out of 100,000 students, 34,229 (34.2%) received an offer, and 65,771 (65.8%) did not.

Code 
counts = data['Offer_Received'].value_counts().sort_index()
labels = ['No Offer', 'Received Offer']
colors = ['#d9534f', '#5cb85c']

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6))

wedges, texts, autotexts = ax.pie(
    counts,
    labels=labels,
    colors=colors,
    autopct='%1.1f%%',
    startangle=90,
    wedgeprops=dict(width=0.5),
    textprops=dict(fontsize=12)
)

for at in autotexts:
    at.set_fontsize(13)
    at.set_fontweight('bold')

ax.text(0, 0, f'n = {len(data):,}', ha='center', va='center',
        fontsize=12, fontweight='bold', color='#444')

ax.set_title('Students Who Received a Job Offer', fontsize=13,
             fontweight='bold', pad=15)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('offer_donut.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

Offer Rate Based On Major and University Tier

This slope chart shows how offer rates shift from Lower-tier to Top-tier across every major simultaneously. We can observe that business students receive the highest offer rates regardless of major and university tier, while healthcare receives the lowest at lower-tier universities, and arts students receive the lowest at mid-tier and top-tier universities.

Code 
data['University_Rating'] = pd.Categorical(
    data['University_Rating'],
    categories=['Lower-tier', 'Mid-tier', 'Top-tier'], ordered=True
)

slope_data = (
    data.groupby(['Major_Category', 'University_Rating'], observed=True)['Offer_Received']
    .mean()
    .round(3)
    .reset_index()
)

majors = sorted(slope_data['Major_Category'].unique())
tiers  = ['Lower-tier', 'Mid-tier', 'Top-tier']
colors = ['#e15759', '#4e79a7', '#f28e2b', '#59a14f', '#b07aa1']

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5))

for i, major in enumerate(majors):
    subset = slope_data[slope_data['Major_Category'] == major].sort_values('University_Rating')
    ax.plot(tiers, subset['Offer_Received'] * 100, color=colors[i],
            linewidth=2.5, marker='o', markersize=7, label=major)

ax.set_title('Offer Rate by Major & University Tier', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_ylabel('Offer Rate (%)')
ax.spines[['top', 'right']].set_visible(False)
ax.legend(title='Major', bbox_to_anchor=(1, 1), loc='upper left', frameon=False)
ax.grid(axis='y', linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('slope_chart.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

Offer Salary Distribution Based On Major

This boxplot shows salary spread by major based on medians, ranges, and outliers all at once. It is sorted low to high by median, so the gap is immediately obvious. STEM sits nearly $20k above Arts/Humanities/Healthcare at the median, and also has the widest spread, meaning high upside but more variance in what you’ll actually land.

Code 
offers = data[data['Offer_Received'] == 1]
order  = offers.groupby('Major_Category')['Offer_Salary'].median().sort_values().index

data2   = [offers[offers['Major_Category'] == m]['Offer_Salary'].values for m in order]
colors = ['#b07aa1', '#e15759', '#f28e2b', '#4e79a7', '#59a14f']

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))

bp = ax.boxplot(data2, patch_artist=True, notch=False,
                medianprops=dict(color='white', linewidth=2))

for patch, color in zip(bp['boxes'], colors):
    patch.set_facecolor(color)
    patch.set_alpha(0.8)

ax.set_xticklabels(order, fontsize=10)
ax.set_title('Offer Salary Distribution by Major', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_ylabel('Offer Salary (USD)')
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(plt.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f'${x:,.0f}'))
ax.spines[['top', 'right']].set_visible(False)
ax.grid(axis='y', linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('salary_boxplot.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

Offer Rate Based On Number of Prior Internships

This is a line plot that shows how the offer rate changes as the number of prior internships increases. Each point on the line represents the average offer rate for students with that many internships, making it easy to see whether having more internship experience leads to a higher chance of receiving a job offer.

Code 
internship_offer = (
    data.groupby('Prior_Internships')['Offer_Received']
    .mean()
    .reset_index()
)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5))

ax.plot(internship_offer['Prior_Internships'],
        internship_offer['Offer_Received'] * 100,
        color='#4e79a7', linewidth=2.5, marker='o', markersize=8)

ax.set_title('Offer Rate by Number of Prior Internships', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_xlabel('Number of Prior Internships')
ax.set_ylabel('Offer Rate (%)')
ax.set_xticks(internship_offer['Prior_Internships'])
ax.spines[['top', 'right']].set_visible(False)
ax.grid(axis='y', linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('internship_line.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

Offer Rate Across GPA Range

This is a bar chart that shows the offer rate for students across four GPA ranges: Below 2.5, 2.5–3.0, 3.0–3.5, and 3.5–4.0. Each bar represents the percentage of students in that GPA range who received a job offer, making it easy to see whether academic performance plays a role in job search success.

Code 
# Bin GPA into ranges
data['GPA_Bin'] = pd.cut(data['GPA'], bins=[0, 2.5, 3.0, 3.5, 4.0],
                        labels=['Below 2.5', '2.5–3.0', '3.0–3.5', '3.5–4.0'])

offer_by_gpa = (
    data.groupby('GPA_Bin', observed=True)['Offer_Received']
    .mean()
    .reset_index()
)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5))

bars = ax.bar(offer_by_gpa['GPA_Bin'], offer_by_gpa['Offer_Received'] * 100,
              color=['#d9534f', '#f0ad4e', '#5bc0de', '#5cb85c'], width=0.5)

for bar, val in zip(bars, offer_by_gpa['Offer_Received']):
    ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, bar.get_height() + 0.4,
            f'{val:.0%}', ha='center', va='bottom', fontsize=11, fontweight='bold')

ax.set_title('Offer Rate by GPA Range', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_xlabel('GPA Range')
ax.set_ylabel('Offer Rate (%)')
ax.set_ylim(0, 50)
ax.spines[['top', 'right']].set_visible(False)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('gpa_offer_rate.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

Final Reflection

This analysis explored job search outcomes among 100,000 college students using data from three major platforms: LinkedIn, Handshake, and Indeed. Across all visualizations, several patterns emerged that reveal both the opportunities and limitations students face in today’s job market.

Platform choice appears to matter less than expected. LinkedIn and Handshake tied at a 37% offer rate while Indeed lagged at 26%, suggesting that targeted platforms outperform high-volume general boards. However, the salary slope chart revealed that university tier consistently amplifies outcomes across every major, with Top-tier students earning higher offer rates regardless of field of study. This implies institutional prestige carries significant weight in hiring decisions.

The salary boxplot showed STEM and Business majors commanding the highest median salaries, while Arts and Humanities trailed by nearly $20,000. The GPA bar chart revealed a steady climb in offer rate as academic performance improved, suggesting that grades do play a measurable role in hiring outcomes. Perhaps most telling was the internship line plot — offer rate rose consistently with each additional internship, reinforcing that hands-on experience is one of the strongest signals students can offer employers.

Perhaps most striking was the overall offer rate: only 34% of students received an offer, meaning nearly two-thirds walked away empty-handed. This underscores that job searching for college students remains highly competitive, and factors like GPA and internship experience appear to drive outcomes far more than platform choice alone.
